moment_text: So I think to me, the biggest wake up call was, uh, when I was 25 years old and, uh, my best friend was killed in a car accident. She was on a trip with her parents and her brother. She was driving. Both her and her dad, uh, passed away on the spot. Her brother and her mother survived. They passed away on my birthday. On my Jewish birthday, which, uh, I don't celebrate anymore. I focus on my, uh, English birthday. And it was, um, you know, one of those defining moments that, you know, there's so much despair, but you can only grow after that. And then I did my journey and there. I recognized a lot of other points in my life when events have shaped, you know, and made me who I am today. But this was the one where it's like, okay, life is short Gila. You have to use your assets, you have to use the brain you were given. There was a little bit of a, like a feeling of a mission. You know, God gave you a brain that you can use, and I felt at this point that I had not really. Lived my potential and it pushed me to, to do more, to apply to business school. Uh, to go to business school, and then to apply to McKinsey and go to McKinsey. And I think this moment really propelled me.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: The moment contains a clear agency signal ('making_decisions') as the speaker describes how the event pushed her to apply to business school and live her potential.

But then, you know, I, I, I, uh, lived in the us, went back to France, um, then moved back to the US to Chicago, and then moved to the US to Israel. You know, I felt no one in my family. Died where they were born because you're always running from one country to the other. And I wanted my kids to have a choice to have a home. Of course, they can go study in France and they speak English, French, and Hebrew, and they can go study in the us, but I wanted them to have a home and to stop this generational homelessness.

moment_text: It was a feeling of, um, restlessness for me and very late in life, I was actually doing a retreat in Thailand, um, trying to reflect on, on, on life, you know, and suddenly I, as I was falling asleep, I remembered a dream that I had as a teenager every night. And it was a dream where I'm going upstairs and the stairs disappear. Every time I step on it, every time I step on the stairs, they disappear. So I can't slow down. I have to run, and I can only survive if I run. 'cause the steps don't stay for more than a second when I touch it. And I realized the, the, the meaning of this dream when I was there in Thailand trying to slow down. And I was like, wow, I'm allowed to slow down Gila. You don't have to run. But it's a message that I have to remind myself every day.

moment_text: When you're in this fear in the survival mode, it's a natural mode, right? It's because we are wired to sense threat. We are wired to survive. It's biology 1 0 1, right? You're in defense mode instead of being in the creative mode, you can't be in creative building optimism if you are staying in that place of fear and survival. Edgar wrote the book on the choice, like I felt like I, I, I had to choose. Mm-hmm. I had to choose to. See things differently and, and, and it's a conscious choice all the time. And of course I fall back into the default and I have to remind myself and, you know, and I am preaching to others and then I have to preach to myself. Right. moment_text: By trauma, by mental health. So I spent a lot of time on this since 2020. I also learned a lot about trauma informed, um, organizations and how trauma shapes organizations. I think that's really interesting. That's, yeah. I'll tell you a bit to talk about it. Yeah. Yeah. So what I mean by that is, um, think about a pharma company that brings a product to market. It's a true story, right? And that product ends up killing people. Now, your entire identity as a pharma company is to help people. And now you brought a product to market that killed people, and it's a huge identity crisis, and you put in place all kinds of defense mechanisms so it doesn't happen again.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: contains emotional signals (grief, shame) within a system interaction (workplace).

moment_text: The same way someone who goes through a relock creates defense mechanisms to protect him or herself. You, you need to learn how to let go of those different, same thing in organizations. And then it creates animosity in the organization because marketing and sales trusted r and d, that the product was a good product and you made me sell a product that killed people. Then lo and behold, you're 10 years later and r and d hasn't come up with a new, new product because they're stuck and they're scared, and they've put in place defense mechanisms and risk management practices, et cetera, that are so high that they will never be able to get another product out if they don't work on that trauma.

moment_text: I like to start by saying that even on individual trauma, we have, we don't know much. We, we started search, you know, researching individual trauma in the eighties. moment_text: It's important for me to say that the trauma response is very intelligent. You are trying to protect yourself. I'm gonna give you a real example. A girl after October 7th, she was a Nova survivor. She couldn't walk, she was paralyzed from the waist down, and they did a bunch of tests, and the answer in the end was, it's all in your head. There is no reason why you shouldn't be able to walk. So she comes out of the hospital, as you can imagine, it was crazy days. I'm not blaming anyone at the hospital because they were all super busy with a lot of things, right? It's all in your head is is is a hard sentence. It shouldn't be like this. And, and what we wanted to explain to her is it's all in your head because your head is amazing, because your brain, she plead dead for so many hours to be able to survive. And so that the terrorists wouldn't find her, that her body helped her. By paralyzing her and making it easier for her to play dead it. Understanding that trauma response is a intelligent response that allows you to survive is the first step to accepting what's happening to you.

Or, you know, I wasn't wearing my seatbelt. And then at some point she felt a tingle, uh, broke into tears from filling the tingle on her leg. At that point, talk therapy was an option. moment_text: And so in the collective, the collective trauma is, is being even less studied. He did a lot of work on collective trauma, studying what happened in, in Germany with Nazi, uh, descendants and, and Jewish descendants. Present, and it's very hard to build a future together if we can't stand together in the present.

moment_text: I'd like to see more public-private partnerships. So I think we've seen a lot of people raise their hands, um, to help on, on the private side. Right. It impacts the Minister of Welfare and Social Affairs, minister of Economy, uh, minister of Defense, obviously with all the soldiers. So I'd love to find ways to have more effective public-private partnerships on some of these. Uh, and I think organizations are doing it. I'm not saying, I'm just saying we need more. And um, and I think there's a lot that the public sector can learn from the private sector. I think the private sector sometimes is very snob about the public sector. Like, oh, they don't know what to do. You know? And, and it's not true. Like public sector is full of amazing people as well, right? And it's more about how do we work together to leverage everybody's strength? And then the other thing is, I wish more people would realize why mental health is so important. You know, it's, we're not doing mental health for the sake of mental health, although it would be worthy anyways, but it's about. Physical health. We have so much high blood pressure and cardiovascular issues in general autoimmune diseases, you know, shingles is all around here right now.
